terms.definition: Supreme, highest, best; from ud (up) — that which transcends level.
That very same — the ancient science. Indicates identity across time: knowledge is not invented but recovered.
Ancient; from purātana — from the earliest times. The paramparā stretches to the horizon.
terms.roots: from purā (formerly, of old) + tana (stretched, extended)

terms.definition: Proclaimed, spoken — from pra-vac; implies formal transmission, not casual speech.
Devotee; one who is devoted (from bhaj, to serve). The active condition: engagement.
Friend; intimate companion (from sac, to accompany). The relational condition: closeness.
Mystery, secret; the hidden thing. In Sāṅkhya: that which requires buddhi-sūkṣma (subtle intellect) to perceive.
Unborn, without beginning; the absolute source
Imperishable self; transcendental body never deteriorating
The Lord, the supreme controller
Standing upon, residing in
By His internal potency/power

The disciplic succession; the traditional method of transmitting spiritual knowledge from guru to disciple. Unlike academic knowledge, transcendental wisdom must be received through this living chain.
The sun-god, deity presiding over the sun in this millennium. Represents the eye of the Lord.
Knowledge, wisdom, especially spiritual knowledge of the Absolute Truth. The mature fruit of yoga practice.
Mature perfection or accomplishment in yoga; the completed state when practice bears its fruit.
Faith, clarity of mind, belief in the goal. The opening condition that makes knowledge accessible.
One who has subdued or controlled the senses; sense withdrawal as prerequisite. The Ekādaśī principle of inner fasting.
Very soon, without delay, quickly. The immediacy of knowledge-peace once conditions are met.
Supreme, transcendental peace; the peace that results from knowledge. Not transactional calm but ontological stillness.
knowledge, wisdom, especially spiritual knowledge of the Absolute Truth; the mature fruit of yoga practice
mature perfection or accomplishment in yoga; the completed state when practice bears its fruit; mature fruit of all mysticism
faith, clarity of mind, belief in the goal; the opening condition that makes knowledge accessible
one who has subdued or controlled the senses; sense withdrawal as prerequisite; the Ekādasi principle of inner fasting embodied
very soon, without delay, quickly; the immediacy of knowledge-peace once conditions are met
supreme, transcendental peace; the peace that results from knowledge; not transactional calm but ontological stillness
whenever and wherever; cyclical, not linear. The Lord manifests repeatedly, not just once.
terms.roots: from yadā (when); reduplication indicating recurrence and universality

terms.definition: of cosmic order, natural law; the supporting structure of existence
terms.roots: from dharma (religious principles, cosmic law) + sya (genitive case suffix)

terms.definition: decline, deterioration, erosion; when peak performance fades
terms.roots: from glai (to become tired, decline, languish)

terms.definition: rising up, predominance, victorious emergence; the error state becoming default
terms.roots: from abhi (toward, upon) + ut (up) + sthā (to stand); standing up against

terms.definition: of irreligion, that which violates natural law
I manifest, emit, release; NOT create — eternal forms become apparent
terms.roots: from sṛj (to release, emit, manifest, discharge)

terms.definition: Myself, personally; direct presence, not delegation
terms.roots: from ātman (self, soul); ātmānam indicates the Supreme Person descends personally

terms.definition: whenever and wherever; cyclical, not linear. The Lord manifests repeatedly, not just once.
terms.roots: from yadā (when); reduplication indicating recurrence and universality

terms.definition: of cosmic order, natural law; the supporting structure of existence
terms.roots: from dharma (religious principles, cosmic law) + sya (genitive case suffix)

terms.definition: decline, deterioration, erosion; when peak performance fades
terms.roots: from glai (to become tired, decline, languish)

terms.definition: rising up, predominance, victorious emergence; the error state becoming default
terms.roots: from abhi (toward, upon) + ut (up) + sthā (to stand); standing up against

terms.definition: of irreligion, that which violates natural law
I manifest, emit, release; NOT create — eternal forms become apparent
terms.roots: from sṛj (to release, emit, manifest, discharge)

terms.definition: Myself, personally; direct presence, not delegation
terms.roots: from ātman (self, soul); ātmānam indicates the Supreme Person descends personally

terms.definition: Surrender to the Lord; highest yoga practice, giving root access to the Divine Administrator.
terms.roots: from īśvara (Supreme Lord) + praṇidhāna (surrender, devotion); from praṇi-dhā (to place before, entrust)

terms.definition: Millennium after millennium; cyclical time, spiral recurrence of divine descents.
Reestablishing cosmic order; restoring the original source code, not inventing new principles.
terms.roots: from dharma (righteousness) + sam (together) + sthā (to stand, establish); making righteousness stand firm again

terms.definition: Miscreant; one who performs evil or forbidden actions, running corrupted guṇas-code unaware of infection.
Annihilation; removal of obstruction, not vengeance—deleting malware to restore integrity.
terms.roots: from vi (apart, away) + naś (to perish, destroy); destruction of falsehood to uphold truth

terms.definition: The pious; one in Kṛṣṇa consciousness, defined functionally rather than by external morality.
Deliverance, rescue; the Lord's primary mission is saving, not destroying.
terms.roots: from pari (around, completely) + trā (to protect, save)

terms.definition: Destruction of intelligence; the collapse of buddhi's capacity to reflect puruṣa. The CPU is fried.
Contemplating, the mind's scanning function; first step in the chain of entanglement. Without redirection toward puruṣa, defaults to viṣaya.
Aversion, repulsion; from dviṣ "to hate". The tendency to push away unpleasant objects; rejection in the guṇa matrix.
Anger; from kru 'to be crooked'; the ego's defense when desire is frustrated. Arises when kāma is obstructed.
Desire, lust; from kam 'to wish'; the extension of saṅga into active seeking—the process spawning beyond control.
Having Me (mat-) as the highest/ultimate (paraḥ); single-pointed devotion. Intent on Kṛṣṇa as the exclusive goal.
Grace, mercy, serene clarity; from pra-sad "to settle down, become clear". The settled state of citta when vṛtti ceases.
Well-founded, established, rooted; wisdom becomes stable like a tree with deep roots. Not fleeting insight but founded understanding.
Is lost, falls; the terminal state of the kleśa cascade. Like gravity—operates whether one is aware or not.
Attachment, coloring toward; from raj "to color". The tendency to grasp at pleasant objects; attraction in the guṇa matrix.
Perfect delusion, complete bewilderment; total loss of orientation in matter. The mirror shaken by tremors.
Restraining, unifying, coalescing; gathering the scattered senses into one direction. Not suppression but unified direction.
Attachment, binding; from saṅj 'to join'; the merge point where buddhi fuses with prakṛti instead of reflecting puruṣa.
Bewilderment of memory; the native memory of spiritual identity becomes distorted. Forgetting who you are.
Liberated, free from; vi-mukta—completely released from the binding force. Not suppression but transcendence.
Yoked, united; one who has accomplished yoga; the conquered mind. The mind yoked to Īśvara, neither scattered nor grasping.

Disciplined practice; repeated application of the mind toward the goal. The effort to be fixed in concentrating the mind (YS 1.13). One of two principal means for restraining mental modifications (YS 1.12).
terms.roots: from abhi (toward) + ās (to sit, to be present)

terms.definition: Steadily situated, immovably established; acala (unmoving) + pratiṣṭha (foundation). The ocean's stability—not rigid, but unshakeable presence. Foundation on all sides.
Uncuttable, unbreakable
Unburnable
Attains, reaches; adhi-√gam (to go toward). The destination achieved through transformation, not travel. Śānti obtained via niḥspṛhaḥ, nirmamaḥ, nirahaṅkāraḥ.
with consciousness fixed on the self — the cognitive mode where awareness acknowledges its source in the Divine, like a wave knowing it is water.
Fire-heat-wind
Ego-function creating identification, appropriating experience as 'mine.' Self-referential pointer mistaking traced memory address for source of consciousness.
Non-action, false renunciation
One who refrains from work; a (without) + karma (action) + kṛt (doer). No such creature exists. Even the stone sits, even the corpse decays. The attempt to not act is itself action under the guṇas.
Insoluble
In the water; the sense-field, the ocean of viṣaya. The field upon which the mind-boat floats, vulnerable to wind.
Liberation, immortality, nectar-state; not merely longevity but freedom from the cycle of birth and death.
Impermanent, non-eternal
At the time of death; anta (end) + kāla (time). The ultimate test - even at death, one is not deluded. The fear that drives worldly activity proves powerless.
Is followed, is obeyed; from anu-vidh (to follow rules). The mind's voluntary submission to sensory movement — the discipline failure. The boat that drifts with the current.
terms.roots: anu (following after) + vidh (to know, lay down rules)

terms.definition: From non-commencement, from not beginning; a (without) + ārambha (beginning/undertaking). The escapist who refuses to act—avoidance is still attachment. Not by mere inaction can one achieve naiṣkarmya.

terms.type: verb, present middle voice 3rd person singular
terms.definition: Begins, undertakes, commences. From ā-rabh: to seize, grasp, begin. The middle voice emphasizes self-benefit through action—the practitioner benefits themselves through devotional engagement.

terms.type: nominalized past passive participle, nominative singular
terms.definition: Unattached, free from clinging; from a-sañj. Freedom from binding desires—not aversion to objects but non-attachment to results. The karmic chain is severed, yet action continues.

Non-attachment; the state of engaging fully in action while recognizing that results belong to prakṛti's operation, not to the witnessing puruṣa. This is not withdrawal or indifference — it is action unburdened by the self-image that demands specific outcomes; hacker metaphor: running processes without claiming ownership of their output.
Wonderful, amazing
Undryable

terms.type: verb, present middle voice 3rd person singular
terms.definition: Sits, remains, dwells. From ās (to sit). The pretender's posture of meditation—body still while mind wanders. The middle voice emphasizes self-benefit that never arrives.
if, however — introduces alternative viewpoint; the pivot from previous arguments to new consideration
Helpless, under compulsion; a (not) + vaśa (control). Free will is NOT doing nothing — it's choosing WHOSE consciousness directs the unavoidable action compelled by prakṛti.
terms.roots: a (not) + vaśa (control, from vaś to wish, desire)

terms.definition: Indestructible; not capable of being destroyed
Unmanifest, invisible, latent; the subtle seed-state before material manifestation emerges from prakṛti; like source code before compilation.
Inexhaustible, never depleted
Attains, reaches, obtains; from aś (to reach, attain). The puruṣa (soul) does not aśnute (attain) naiṣkarmya by avoidance. Active engagement required.
They worship, serve, share in, enjoy. From bhaj (to share, serve). The verb of loving devotion—active engagement with the Divine. Not contemplation but participatory connection.
By devotion, with devotion. The instrumental means of connection—devotion is not the goal but the tool, the method, the instrument that opens the heart to divine reciprocation.
Fixed mind, steadiness, cultivation; the citta stabilized in its object. The process locked in memory. Without yukti, even the capacity for steady-mindedness is absent.
Created being, elemental manifestation; from √bhū, 'to become, to come into being'—the evolutes of prakṛti.
Living entities, beings; literally 'that which has come into existence' (from bhū — to be). All embodied souls, all beings who have manifested from the primordial.
Divine, pertaining to Brahman; the state of being established in ultimate reality. The transformed condition of consciousness - neither technique nor practice but being.
Intelligence, discernment; the interface between puruṣa and prakṛti. When pure sattva dominates, capable of recognizing the soul's true nature.
Of those that roam/wander; from car (to move). The senses in their natural uncontrolled state — not evil, just mobile. The wind that blows; the problem is only when the mind follows.
They cut, sever, split
Steady, patient, firm; one who holds steady under pressure—the carrier of spiritual weight without system collapse.
Pain, distress, suffering; "hard ground," the existential dissatisfaction of material existence.
Pairs of opposites; hot-cold, pleasure-pain, honor-dishonor—the oscillating expressions of prakṛti that disturb the unsteady mind.
Hateful, odious, one who is to be hated. From dviṣ (to hate). The Lord declares no one is hateful to Him—hatred finds no target in the Absolute.
Two-fold, of two kinds; dvi (two) + vidhā (manner/kind). Kṛṣṇa's answer to Arjuna's demand for ONE path: there are TWO classes of men, two approaches to the same truth.
This; referring to the previously described state. The brahmī sthitiḥ - the divine state just elaborated through verses 2.66-2.71.
Terrible, dreadful, fierce; the quality of the battlefield, the difficult act. Karmaṇi ghore—in terrible, fearsome action. Not escape, but courageous engagement.
Three constituent qualities of prakṛti: sattva (lucidity), rajas (activity), tamas (inertia). All material phenomena are combinations of these three.
By the modes (sattva, rajas, tamas); guṇa = quality, strand. The three strands of prakṛti that weave all embodied action. One acts helplessly according to which guṇas dominate.
Destruction, cessation; not suppression but natural dissolving when cause is removed. When prasāda is present, the threefold miseries undergo natural hāniḥ — they cease to register.
Objects of the senses. Indriya (sense) + artha (object/purpose) + ān (accusative plural). The viṣaya that the pretender's mind secretly craves while body remains still.
The five senses of perception (jñānendriyas): sight, hearing, touch, taste, smell. The instruments through which prakṛti delivers sensory data. Not suppressed but regulated by the mind.
terms.roots: from indriya (belonging to Indra, sensory power)

terms.definition: From the objects of the senses; indriya (sense) + artha (object, purpose) + ablative plural suffix. What is restrained is contact with viṣaya—not the senses themselves but their engagement with objects.
Worn out, old, useless
Superior, better, more excellent
Better, superior; the comparative of jyāya (elder/greater). Arjuna's question: is knowledge better than action? The eternal seeker's dilemma.
Better, superior — comparative excellence. When duty is performed consciously, it exceeds attempted transcendence.

The yoga of knowledge; jñāna (knowledge) + yoga (union/discipline). The path of Sāṅkhyas, analytical understanding of spirit and matter. The indirect process leading eventually to Kṛṣṇa consciousness.
Is awake, is alert; from jāgṛ (to wake, be vigilant). Not biological wakefulness but conscious presence. The saṁyamī is jāgarti in the night of material existence.
Is considered eligible, fit, suitable; the technical term for 'qualified practitioner'—having the prerequisites for liberation.
terms.roots: from √kḷp (to be able, to be suitable, to arrange)

terms.definition: Action, work; has layers—fruitive (binding), prescribed (purifying), karma-yoga (liberating). Requires viveka to discriminate between them.
Organs of action: hands (pāṇi), legs (pāda), speech (vāk), genitals (upastha), anus (pāyu); the five instruments of outward movement.
The yoga of action; karma (action) + yoga (discipline). Working in Kṛṣṇa consciousness, the path of devotional service. The direct process that is simultaneously easy and sublime.
The path of duty without attachment; the yoga of action dedicated to the Supreme Viṣṇu. The bridge between saṃsāra and mokṣa. Ordinary work becomes worship when offered to the Lord.
By the organs of action; the five karmendriyas: hands, legs, speech, genitals, and anus. The active instruments through which prakṛti expresses. Engaged in yoga rather than suppressed.
The five organs of action (hands, legs, speech, genitals, anus) having been controlled/restrained. The external show of physical restraint that defines the pretender.
Subject performing action. In reality, only prakṛti through guṇas is agent. Soul claims role erroneously.
O Kṛṣṇa; keśa (hair) + va (possessor); literally 'one with beautiful hair'. Arjuna's address: the intimate, the accessible, the beautiful Lord.
They moisten, wet
One who desires to fulfill desires; the tautology reveals the futility. Desire chasing desire creates an infinite loop, presuming lack where the ocean finds completion.
Lust personified; desire incarnate; the 'eternal enemy... insatiable as fire'. It assumes a form (rūpa) in the mind of the embodied soul.
Is compelled, is caused to act; passive of kṛ (to do). The self is kāryate by the guṇas. One acts helplessly — not freely choosing inaction, but helplessly driven by the modes.
tortoise; the eternal symbol of deliberate withdrawal. Not rejection — retractability; hacker metaphor: hot-swappable modules: attached when needed, detached when not
terms.roots: kūrma (tortoise, one of the divine incarnations)

terms.definition: Moment, instant; from kṣaṇ (to wink). 'Not even for a moment' — the guṇas compel constantly. No creature can refrain from action, not even for the smallest unit of time.
mighty-armed — ironic address; Arjuna's physical might contrasts with mental weakness
By the mind; the faculty of consciousness between ahaṅkāra and buddhi. The integration point of incoming sensory data. The instrument through which senses are regulated toward yoga.
Remembering within the mind; mental dwelling upon sense-objects even during physical restraint. The background process consuming your CPU cycles while you think the main thread is suspended.
Ego's primary activity: mental fabrication, constructing narratives of authorship.
Thought, opinion, doctrine; from man (to think). Your considered view, O Kṛṣṇa—the teaching, the position taken.
Pretender, hypocrite. Mithyā (false) + ācāraḥ (conduct/behavior). The greatest cheater—one whose external show contradicts internal reality. Body restrained, mind wandering.
you do not deserve — the verdict: grief is beneath the station of a warrior
Freedom from reaction, inaction in action; the state of being akarma. Not mere absence of action but action without binding consequence. Cannot be achieved by anārambhāt (non-beginning).
Annihilation, dissolution, death; the return to unmanifest state; not destruction but transformation.
terms.roots: ni (down, into) + dhana (putting, placing); √dhā (to place, put)

terms.type: past passive participle, nominative plural neuter
terms.definition: Restrained, controlled, held down; from ni-grah (to hold down, check). Not suppression but directed holding—wild horses reined, not blindfolded. Pratyāhāra as willed direction.
Devoid of false ego; nir (without) + ahaṅkāra (I-sense, ego). The root of bondage cut. The 'I' that appropriates experience dissolved into the witnessing puruṣa.
Without sense of 'mine'; nir (without) + mama (mine). No appropriation, no sense of proprietorship. The 'me' and 'mine' that bind one to karma dissolved.
Extinction, liberation from material existence; nir + vāṇa (blowing out). Not extinction of existence but of bondage. The culmination - paramaṁ (supreme).
without expectation — acting without attachment to specific results. The sky doesn't rain expecting gratitude; the sun doesn't shine for appreciation.
eternally born — the materialist doctrine of perpetual re-generation without permanent soul
Regulating, controlling, directing—not suppressing. From ni-yam: the active governance of the senses by the mind. Not violent suppression but skillful direction toward the Supreme.
Prescribed, regulated, ordained
Prescribed, ordained, regulated — the specific, non-negotiable duty encoded for one's station and capacity.

terms.type: verb, causative present active 2nd person singular
terms.definition: You cause to engage, you employ, you urge; from ni-yuj (to yoke). The question: why do you compel me? The causative implies making someone do something.
terms.roots: from ni-yuj (to engage, employ) — causative form

terms.definition: Night, darkness, state of unknowing; the condition where consciousness is obscured. For material beings: night is spiritual perception. For the sage: night is material existence. Complete perceptual inversion.
Free from desire; from nir + spṛh (to wish). Natural state without craving. The vitṛṣṇa of YS 1.15 made manifest—devoid of thirst for seen and heard objects.
Firm standing, devotion, faith, fixed concentration; from ni-sthā (to stand firmly). Steadiness in one's chosen path, not wavering between methods.
Boat, vessel, ship; the mind as vehicle. Requires direction or drifts. The wind of senses can sweep it away unless anchored by pratyāhāra.

The Supreme; the ultimate reality. In dualistic Sankhya, this refers to the highest attainment — union with puruṣa, liberation from the cycle of birth and death. Not a distant abstraction, but the direct recognition of oneself as pure consciousness; hacker metaphor: root access to the system, the highest privilege level.
Lamentation, excessive grieving, crying out; the emotional reaction fired by ahaṅkāra's misidentification with temporary forms.

Stands firmly all around, becomes well-established; pari (completely, around) + atiṣṭhate (stands firm). Buddhi becomes established like a house with foundations on all sides — stable from every angle.
He sees
Fire, purifier

terms.type: present active participle, genitive singular masculine
terms.definition: Of one who sees, of the seer; from paś (to see). The sage whose perception is purified — sees what the materialist misses. The witness-function of puruṣa.
Wisdom, intelligence, discriminative knowledge; from pra + jñā (to know). The capacity for discrimination — the CPU of consciousness. The cargo swept away when the mind-boat drifts.
Primordial material substance; unconscious substrate containing three guṇas.
terms.roots: from pra (before) + kṛ (to do, to make)

terms.definition: Born of material nature, produced by prakṛti; ja = born. The guṇas (sattva, rajas, tamas) are born of prakṛti — and by them, all are compelled to act.
Of the satisfied/clear/placid mind; prasanna = placid, clear, satisfied + cetas = mind/heart. When the mind's waters become still and clear, reflection of puruṣa becomes possible.
Would be accomplished, effected
Would be accomplished, effected, successful. Without work, not even bodily maintenance succeeds.

In the settled state of grace; when citta settles into the ocean of consciousness. The prerequisite for hāniḥ — the destruction of miseries. Not conventional happiness but the condition in which buddhi becomes well-established.
terms.roots: from pra-sad (to settle down, become clear)

terms.definition: Established, founded, standing firm; from prati-sthā (to stand firm). Stability achieved—not stability sought but accomplished. The boat now anchored, not drifting.
Withdrawal of the senses from their objects; the fifth limb of aṣṭāṅga yoga. Not suppression but redirected flow — indriyāṇām following cittasya toward svarūpa.
terms.roots: prati (back, against) + ā-hṛ (to withdraw, draw back)

terms.definition: Direct perception of the soul; the discriminative knowledge that transforms theoretical Sāṅkhya into realized truth.

A person; human being; spirit as individual. Here used to indicate that the teaching applies to anyone — man, woman, any varṇa, any stage of life. Yoga is not restricted. The mechanics work for anyone who applies them.
That person; he who practices thus with sincerity. The demonstrative pointing to the one who performs karma-yoga with non-attachment. Not just anyone, but the true practitioner.
Equal, same, balanced, equanimous; the quality of buddhi when sattva predominates and rajas/tamas are subdued.
One fully attains; sam (together) + adhi (over, toward) + gam (to go). The complete going-toward that results in siddhi. Not partial, not incomplete, but thorough attainment.
Equally disposed, same, impartial, level. The Lord's equal disposition toward all beings—no differentiation or preference. Not emotional neutrality but ontological equality.
Ocean, the gathering-place of waters; sam (together) + udra (water). Not merely large volume but wholeness. Nothing can be added to or taken from that which is already full.
He acquires, obtains, enters
From renunciation; sannyāsa = complete renunciation of world and works. Not by outer show alone. The monk who left the world but not the ego has gone nowhere. True renunciation is inner.
terms.roots: sam (together, thoroughly) + nyas (to place down) — to put down completely

terms.definition: having fully renounced — complete surrender of agency, not abandonment of action. Like a symphony musician surrendering to the conductor.
terms.roots: sam (completely) + ni (down) + as (to throw, cast off)

terms.definition: In all living entities. Sara (all) + bhūta (living being, entity). The comprehensive scope of the Lord's equal presence—no being is excluded from divine presence.
All-pervading, omnipresent
In all respects, completely, totally; sarva (all) + śaḥ (direction/way). Total restraint—not partial, not selective. All senses directed toward puruṣa.
Weapons, instruments
winds up, withdraws; the total retraction of attention; hacker metaphor: disconnecting from the simulation without shutting down the machine
terms.roots: from √hṛ (to take, carry) with prefix sam- (together, completely)

terms.definition: Having steadied; having rendered stable; having fixed firmly. Refers to the deliberate action of stabilizing the mental apparatus (mind, intelligence) through superior discrimination.
terms.roots: from sam (intensive prefix) + stambh (to fix, to establish)

terms.definition: One who practices self-control; literally 'one who holds together'. The yogi who holds the mind from its natural tendency to fragment. From saṁyama — the integrated practice of dhāraṇā, dhyāna, samādhi.
Thinking of, dwelling upon, remembering. From smṛ (to remember). The smoking gun of the pretender—the mind actively engaging with sense objects while body feigns renunciation.
He dries up
He hears
State, position, establishment; from sthā (to stand). The standing firm - not achieved, not sought, but realized. The seer established in its own nature.
Fixed, immovable
Happiness, pleasure, ease; "good ground"—the agreeable feeling-state that still binds the enjoyer to prakṛti.

Happiness, ease, comfort; the fruit that requires śānti as prerequisite. The output impossible without proper input chain. Without peace, there can be no happiness — the question is rhetorical: kutaḥ sukham?
terms.roots: from su (good) + kha (axle-hole, space for axle to turn smoothly)

terms.definition: Of the Sāṅkhyas, the empiric philosophers; followers of Sāṅkhya who analyze spirit and matter. Philosophers honestly seeking the Absolute Truth will eventually come to Kṛṣṇa consciousness.
Therefore, from that, hence; logical connector establishing causality. BG 2.67's destruction (boat swept away) leads to 2.68's prescription (restraint leading to establishment).
Pervaded, spread throughout
even then, still — the crux: regardless of philosophical position, conclusions remain unchanged
Forbearance, endurance
But; on the other hand. The contrasting particle that marks the dramatic shift from BG 3.6's false yogī to the genuine practitioner. The pivot point of the teaching.
Is called, is named, is said. From vac (to speak). The passive voice emphasizes external judgment—such a person IS CALLED a pretender by the wise.
Equanimity, overlooking
He speaks
Dispassion; dispossession; indifference toward sense objects. Freedom from coloration (rāga) of the mind by objects. Strategic withdrawal of attention from objects that trigger the kleśas.
Garments, clothes, coverings
Know! Understand!
without fever — the absence of nervous mental agitation about outcomes. The body works; the mind calculates; but the grip of anxiety dissolves.
Having abandoned, having given up; the active completion of renunciation. Not suppression or denial, but total release. The hands must open to receive fullness.
State of confusion by false identification. Confusion caused by ahaṅkāra making soul think it is doer.
One whose self is deluded/bewildered. Vimūḍha (deluded) + ātmā (self). Not merely confused but thoroughly deluded—vi (intensive) + mūḍha. The pretender's true state.
Becomes deluded; vi-muh (to be thoroughly confused). Negated with na - never deluded. The kleśa cascade cannot touch one established here.
Is by far superior; is distinguished, surpasses all others. From vi-śiṣ: to distinguish, surpass. The passive voice indicates divine recognition—this person is marked as superior by the Lord Himself.
Manifest, visible, expressed; the material form appearing in interim time; the compiled program running in the simulation.
Obstacle yoga: An inauspicious combination meaning 'falling away.' The prompt to act with extra care, as wrong action now carries amplified consequence.
By speech, by words; from vac (to speak). The instrumental through which buddhi is bewildered. It is not Kṛṣṇa's intention but Arjuna's reception that creates vyāmiśreṇa (confusion).
One who; the subject being described. Inclusive of any earnest practitioner regardless of external position. BG 3.7 begins with this inclusive opening—anyone can be the superior yogī.
Of the yogīs, of those devoted to yoga practice; working in Kṛṣṇa consciousness. The direct bhaktas who arrive at the destination without the winding road of philosophical speculation.
Under self-control; the ātma as commander, not prisoner. Self-mastery through regulation, not will-power.

terms.type: present passive participle, accusative singular neuter
terms.definition: Always being filled, constantly replenished; from ā-pṝ (to fill completely). The ocean's quality of receiving incessantly without agitation. Rivers flow in; the ocean remains still.
terms.roots: ā (completely) + pṝ (to fill, from pṝ to be full)

terms.definition: Of one who is not connected; a-yukta = without yukti (union/devotion). The disconnected node in the network. Without connection to the Supreme, neither transcendental intelligence nor steady mind is possible.
surrender to the Lord — the yogic practice of offering all actions to the Divine. The accelerator of spiritual practice.
Bodily maintenance, the journey of the body — the minimum viable obligation. Even the embodied soul requires the vehicle's upkeep.
Peace, tranquility; cessation of vṛtti agitation. The system at rest. Without bhāvanā (steady mind), there can be no śānti. The prerequisite chain: yukti → buddhi → bhāvanā → śānti → sukha.
Duty, what must be done. Not optional work. The non-negotiables of your dharma.
Constantly. Not when inspired. Without interruption.
Perform completely. Execute fully.
terms.roots: from sam (wholly, utterly) + ācar (to perform, practice)

terms.definition: Divine, transcendental; of the nature of divinity, not material manifestation. The Lord's birth and activities are divyam—free from material conditioning.
In truth, in reality; ontologically, not superficially or philosophically. Knowledge of the Lord's nature must be tattvataḥ—realized, not merely intellectual.
Again taking birth; the cycle of saṁsāra perpetuated by residual karma and ignorance. The one who knows divyam-janma escapes punar-janma.
Does not attain; negation of return. The liberated soul does not naiti (attain/return to) the material cycle—cessation of rebirth.
Attains Me; personal attainment of the Supreme Person. Not void or impersonal dispersion, but direct presence with Kṛṣṇa.
Freed, released from; indicating completed liberation from binding conditions
Attachment, passion, desire for pleasure; one of the three gates of hell (BG 16.21); grasping after pleasant experiences
Fear, anxiety, dread of unpleasant; binding through avoidance and aversion
Anger, wrath, defensive reaction; destructive emotional response that arises from frustrated attachment
Made of Me, absorbed in Me; mind saturated with Kṛṣṇa-consciousness to the point of identification
Having taken refuge, surrendered; those who have sought shelter in the Supreme
terms.roots: upa (near) + śri (to lean on, rest upon)

terms.definition: By the austerity/heat of knowledge; purification through discriminative understanding that burns away ignorance
My state of being, My nature; transcendental love and the Lord's own prema-bhāva which is the destination of knowledge
Approaching by bowing down; complete surrender before the spiritual master.
Inquiry from all sides; submissive questioning.
Service; selfless action without desire for result.
One who sees the truth; self-realized soul.
Instruction; pointing out the truth.
Detached, unattached.
Non-eternal, impermanent.
Disciplic succession; unbroken chain of transmission.
Received; obtained through transmission.
Saintly kings; rulers established in dharma.
O scorcher of enemies; Arjuna's epithet.
Scattered, lost; access severed, not truth destroyed.
In the course of great time; entropy on lineages.
They knew; direct perception, not theory.
